title: Chapter 6: The Garden
content: "Come walk with me."

Edward Rochester said it like a suggestion. It was not a suggestion. Jane had learned, in four days at Thornfield, that his suggestions arrived with the gravitational certainty of weather — you could note them, you could resent them, but you did not refuse them.

She set down the calico she'd been draping on the workshop form. He stood in the atelier doorway with the particular stillness of a man who wanted to be looked at — one hand in his pocket, his body angled toward the window light. Linen shirt the color of wet sand, sleeves rolled. Everything he wore was Thornfield. Jane wondered if he'd ever put on another house's garment and felt the betrayal like infidelity.
"Where?" she asked.
"The garden maze." He smiled — small, conspiratorial. "The one place in this château without cameras. Even Orwell needed a corner without eyes."

Jane registered this the way she registered a seam that didn't match its pattern: precisely. He was choosing a private space. He was telling her he was choosing a private space. The transparency was itself a technique — give someone the obvious manipulation so they stop looking for the subtle one.
She followed him anyway.

The garden began where the gravel terrace ended. Late September had been kind to the Loire Valley, and the maze entrance was flanked by roses still holding their color — deep burgundy heads, heavy with their own weight, nodding like drowsy aristocrats. The air smelled of warm soil and something older underneath, the mineral breath of ground that had been turned and planted and turned again for centuries. Beneath the roses, beneath the green, the faint tang of rot. Not unpleasant. The smell of a garden processing its own dead.

The hedges were yew, clipped to eight feet, dense as walls. Edward pulled a tin gate open — its hinges groaned, green with age — and gestured her inside with a half-bow that would have been mocking on anyone else. On him, it landed somewhere between gallant and proprietary, as though he were admitting her to a room he'd built for exactly this purpose.

"Do you know the trick of mazes?" he asked, falling into step beside her on the gravel path. Their shoes crunched in soft unison. "You don't solve them by looking ahead. You solve them by putting one hand on the wall and following it. The hand never leaves the surface. Eventually, it carries you to the center."
"That sounds like a metaphor for something," Jane said.
"Everything is a metaphor for something." He glanced at her sidelong. "I'd have thought a woman who reads garments for a living would know that."

The path narrowed. The hedges pressed closer, and the sounds of the château — distant kitchen clatter, Grace Poole's radio drifting from some upper window — were swallowed by the green walls. The light came through the yew in dappled shafts, gilding the gravel ahead in shifting coin. Jane's shoulders dropped half an inch without her permission. The maze was beautiful, and beauty was a kind of disarming. She noted it. Noted it and kept walking.
Edward spoke first, and his voice had changed. Lower. The charm was still there, but it had moved to the background like a bass note, while something rawer took the melody.

"My family were textile industrialists," he said. "Three generations of Rochesters who treated workers like interchangeable parts on a loom. The people who made the cloth were just another raw material." He paused to let a late bee pass between them, its drone filling the silence. "I grew up in a house where the servants had separate staircases. Separate lives that never intersected with ours except in function. You can't unsee that, once you see it."
Jane said nothing. She let the silence do what silences do — fill with whatever the speaker needed it to hold.

"I rebelled," he continued, and the word carried the faint thrill of confession. "I wanted to build something that was the opposite of everything they stood for. Thornfield was supposed to be proof that luxury could be honest. That you could charge two thousand euros for a coat and still credit the hands that made it. Still pay a living wage. Still..." He trailed off. The path curved left, and he followed it without seeming to think about the direction. "Still be worthy of the craft."

His voice on the word *worthy* — Jane felt it in her sternum like a low vibration. The yearning was real. That was the devastating thing. He DID wish he were the man he was describing. She could hear it, the same way she could feel the difference between a genuine hand-felled seam and a machine stitch pretending to be one: the irregularity that proved the human hand had been there. Edward Rochester's hunger to be good was not fake. It was just... unlived. A sketch that had never been cut into cloth.

She almost believed him. Almost. Because the yearning was real, and real yearning in a voice like his was a seduction that bypassed the body entirely and went straight for her own unfinished longings.

The path forked. Edward took the right branch without hesitation. He knew this maze by heart, of course. He'd designed it, or commissioned it, or at the very least had walked it enough times to have memorized its grammar. Jane filed that away: a man who led you through a space he already controlled and called it a walk.

He stopped at the next junction and turned to face her. The light fell differently here — stronger, as if the hedges had thinned, letting the late-afternoon sun spill through in earnest. It caught the edges of his face and softened the architecture of his features into something that looked, for a moment, like vulnerability.
"I've been looking for someone with your... texture," he said. He paused on the word, tasting it. "Someone unformed. Diamond in the rough, they call it."
The word *unformed* landed like a seam ripper.

Jane didn't flinch. She laughed — a short, bright sound that bounced off the yew and came back to her. "Unformed? Edward, I've been forming myself since I was fourteen. Every piece I wear is a choice. Every opinion I have is earned. I walked into this industry with nothing but a seam ripper and a phone camera, and I built a platform by telling the truth about people who lie for a living." She met his eyes. "If that's unformed, then we're using different dictionaries."
Something shifted behind his expression. A recalculation — she'd seen it before, in the corridor outside the archive. But this time, something else flickered underneath: genuine pleasure.
"Exactly the kind of spirit worth... refining," he said.

*Refining.* The word replaced *unformed* the way one fabric substitutes for another in a sample — same silhouette, different hand-feel. Different word, same implication: he would shape her. He would take the raw material of her and improve it. The generosity of the offer was the trap.
Jane heard it clearly. She heard it the way she heard a blended fiber masquerading as pure silk — the tell is in the burn test, and she would always, always burn test.
He reached for her hand.

It was not a romantic gesture. This was a claiming. He lifted her hand between them the way a tailor lifts a bolt of fabric to check its weight, and he studied her fingers with an attention that bordered on cataloguing.

The calluses on her fingertips were raised and rough — years of handling vintage cloth, of gripping shears and pressing seams, of the particular friction that builds when fingertips meet fabric hundreds of times a day. He turned her hand over, traced the ridge along her index finger, and his thumb circled the pad of her palm with slow, proprietary attention.
"You have worker's hands," he said. Half-amused, half-approving. "Beautiful. Practical. I could build something with hands like these."
The warmth of his grip traveled up her wrist. The September air was cool against the back of her hand where it was exposed, and the contrast — his heat, the garden's chill — sharpened everything.
"You could," she said. "But I'd be building it too."

His eyes met hers, and he smiled — the smile of a man who found resistance charming rather than threatening, who looked at a woman who'd just corrected the terms of their equation and thought: *interesting*, the way a sculptor thinks it when the clay pushes back. He hadn't met real resistance yet. Jane knew this. She let him think he'd found a spirited piece of raw material.
He released her hand. She felt the absence of his grip like a fabric lifted from the cutting table — the sudden cool where warmth had been.
The maze opened.

They emerged into a circular clearing, and there it was: the center. A stone bench, weathered to the color of bone, sat against the far hedge. Beside it, a fountain — a cherub with a cracked urn, the basin dry, a rind of ancient mineral deposit crusting the stone lip. Water hadn't flowed here in years, maybe decades, but the structure remained, stubborn and purposeless, a monument to something that had once worked and now simply existed.

Edward crossed to the bench and sat. The stone received him without complaint. He looked up at her, and his hand patted the space beside him — the gesture of a man who expected the offer to be accepted.
Jane remained standing.

She didn't decide to stand. Her body simply refused the invitation the way it refused garments that didn't fit — a gut-level rejection that bypassed analysis entirely. She stood with her arms loose at her sides and looked down at him, and something in the geometry rearranged itself. He seated, her standing. Him looking up, her looking down. The power inverted, just for a moment.
And then — she saw it.

The performance dropped. Not a calculated bit of vulnerability served up like the story about his family. This was the mask slipping, not being removed. His shoulders dropped. The fine musculature around his eyes relaxed, and underneath the charm, underneath the ceaseless management of every interaction — she saw exhaustion. Genuine exhaustion. The kind that accumulates in people who can never stop performing because they no longer know where the performance ends and the person begins.
"Do you ever get tired of reading people, Jane?" he asked. His voice was quiet, stripped of its usual music. "Knowing what they want before they do?"

The question was too precise to be rhetorical. He was actually asking. And in the asking, he revealed something he hadn't meant to: that he read people the same way she did, compulsively, defensively, because the alternative was to be read first and found wanting.
"Every day," she said. Softly. Because it was true.

Something passed between them — a current, a recognition, the mutual click of two locks made by the same smith. Two people who saw too much and wished they could stop seeing. Two people who knew the cost of constant vigilance.
It was the most dangerous moment yet.

Because real connection with a narcissist is the hook that keeps you. The moment of authenticity makes the manipulation survivable. It gives you proof that the person underneath is worth saving — that if you just stay long enough, love hard enough, the real moments will multiply and the performance will recede. It is the cruelest trick in the repertoire, and it works because it is not, technically, a trick. The loneliness is real. The exhaustion is real. He IS lonely. He DOES want someone to see him. He just can't experience connection without controlling it, can't be seen without staging the lighting, can't be loved without engineering the terms.

Jane felt the hook catch — in her tightening chest, in her dry throat, in the way she wanted — genuinely, stupidly, self-destructively wanted — to sit on that cold stone bench beside him and tell him she understood.
She stayed standing.
The moment passed. The mask reconstructed itself, and Edward smiled up at her — warm, gracious, fully restored. "Shall we head back? The light's going."

She nodded. He rose from the bench, and they retraced the maze's paths toward the gate, his hand finding the yew wall the way he'd told her — one point of contact, following the structure home. The gravel crunched. The roses nodded. The garden processed its dead in silence.

At the gate, he turned. The late sun caught him full in the face, and his eyes were amber, almost feral in the warm light. He lifted her hand again — the same hand, the worker's hand — and brought it to his lips.

The kiss landed on her knuckles. Warm, deliberate, lingering. She felt the pressure of his mouth, the slight moisture of his lower lip, the heat of his breath held against her skin for one beat longer than courtesy required. A sealing gesture. A branding.
He released her hand and held her gaze for a moment, then turned and walked back toward the château without looking back.

Jane stood at the garden gate. The warmth of his lips persisted on her knuckles. She pressed her hand to her cheek, felt the residual heat transfer, and thought: *That was well done. Perfectly done. And I almost fell for the humanity underneath the handiwork.*

Walking back through the terraced gardens toward the château's east entrance, she replayed the conversation with the obsessive precision she brought to garment analysis. *Unformed.* The word that revealed his framework: she was material, not maker. *Refining.* The correction that confirmed it: he would improve her, shape her, take credit for her becoming. The almost-real moment in the maze center. The genuine exhaustion. Her own heartbeat, responding to his loneliness like a tuning fork struck by its twin.
He doesn't want a partner. He wants a raw material. Something to cut and drape and stitch into the shape he's already drawn. And right now, she was letting him think she might be that something.
She reached her room. Closed the door. Leaned against it.

Her laptop sat on the writing desk, screen dark. She opened it on instinct, not expectation — the Wi-Fi at Thornfield was intermittent, a signal that wandered in and out like a guest who couldn't decide whether to stay. But tonight, for whatever reason — a satellite shift, Grace Poole's radio interfering with some ancient router in the basement — the network icon blinked to life.
One notification. One email. From Helen.
The subject line was blank. The body was three words: *Found something. Read.*

The attachment was a dossier. Jane downloaded it with fingers that trembled slightly — not from fear, from the tension of a thread about to pull. The file opened. Photographs first. A woman — dark skin, wild hair pulled back in a scarf that had slipped half-loose, fierce eyes that stared into the camera with the directness of someone who had never learned to perform gratitude she didn't feel. She was standing in what looked like a workshop, a bolt of fabric over one shoulder, shears in her hand. She was laughing. The laugh was wide and unguarded and real.

Jane's chest seized. She knew that posture — the weight of the fabric, the angle of the shears. She knew it the way she knew a hand-felled seam from a fused one: by the irreducible evidence of a maker's body.
The file name read: *Bertha Mason — former Thornfield designer. DISAPPEARED 5 years ago.*

Jane stared at the face on her screen. The woman who laughed in the workshop. The hands that held the shears. The ghost she'd been hearing in the basement, in the space between sounds, in the construction details that whispered *I was here* from inside every early Thornfield garment.
The ghost had a name.
Bertha Mason.
And Helen had found her.
